subject: Statistics
title: The Monty Hall Problem
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on The Monty Hall Problem.

hook.text: Three doors: one car, two goats. You pick door 1. The host opens door 3 — a goat. Should you switch?

buildup.text: Most people say it doesn't matter. But switching wins 2/3 of the time. It's one of the most famous puzzles.

discovery.text: Bayes explains it: the host's action gives you information. It shifts probability from the opened door to the other.

twist.text: Even Paul Erdős, one of the greatest mathematicians, refused to believe it until shown a computer simulation.

climax.text: The Monty Hall problem proves that information changes probability — the core of Bayesian thinking.

punchline.text: New information changes the odds. Always switch.

quiz.question: In the Monty Hall problem, why should you switch doors?
options: The host's reveal shifts probability — switching gives you a 2/3 chance of winning
Switching makes no difference — both doors have equal odds
The host always reveals the car's location
